coredata.dc:title: A sensitive and selective sensing platform based on CdTe QDs in the presence of l-cysteine for detection of silver, mercury and copper ions in water and various drinks
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Tingting
Liu, Junfeng
Liu, Xinxin
Liu, Jie
Xiang, Jinkun
Wu, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: Water soluble CdTe quantum dots (QDs) have been prepared simply by one-pot method using potassium tellurite as stable tellurium source and thioglycolic acid (TGA) as stabilizer. The fluorescence of CdTe QDs can be improved 1.3-fold in the presence of l-cysteine (Cys), however, highly efficiently quenched in the presence of silver or mercury or copper ions. A sensitive and selective sensing platform for analysis of silver, mercury and copper ions has been simply established based on CdTe QDs in the presence of l-cysteine. Under the optimum conditions, excellent linear relationships exist between the quenching degree of the sensing platform and the concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ranging from 0.5 to 40ngmL−1. By using masking agents of sodium diethyldithiocarbamate (DDTC) for Ag+ and Cu2+, NH4OH for Ag+ and Hg2+ and 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) for Hg2+ and Cu2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ can be exclusively detected in coexistence with other two ions, and the detection limits (3σ) were 0.65, 0.063 and 0.088ngmL−1 for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. This effective sensing platform has been used to detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ in water and various drinks with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cys-CdTe QDs
Fluorescence spectrofluorometer
Silver, mercury and copper ions
Water and tea infusion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of water-soluble CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs)
Fluorescence assay of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
Characterization and properties of CdTe QDs
Establishing and optimization the sensing platform (Cys-CdTe QDs)
Effect of amino acid on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs
Effect of CdTe QDs volume on the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs
Fluorescence assay of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
The possible mechanism of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions detection by Cys-CdTe QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra of (a) TGA, (b) TGA-capped CdTe QDs. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CdTe QDs, λex =458nm and λem =560nm. Inset (from left to right): photographs of CdTe QDs taken under visible light and 365nm UV light, respectively.

Effect of various metals on the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs. [Mn+]: 40ngmL−1; Cys: 2.0μgmL−1; CdTe QDs: 35μL. “F/F0” represents the final fluorescence intensity/initial fluorescence intensity.

Relative fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs. The concentration of each analyzed metal is 40ngmL−1, while the other two interfering metals is 10ngmL−1. DDTC, NH4OH and PAN are 0.4μgmL−1, 1.1mmolL−1 and 0.5μgmL−1, respectively. “F/F0” as the same in Fig. 2.

Fluorescence emission spectrum of Cys-CdTe QDs in the present of different concentrations of (0–20.0ngmL−1) Ag+ (A), (0–15.0ngmL−1) Hg2+ (B), and (0.5–40ngmL−1) Cu2+ (C), respectively. The insets show a plot of F0/F versus the corresponding concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. λex =458nm. “F0/F” represents the initial fluorescence intensity/final fluorescence intensity.
Comparison of this work with various reported methods.

entry.italic: Comparison of this work with the same kinds of fluorescence methods

row.entry: Environmental water samples and human hair

entry.italic: Comparison of this work with other kinds of methods

row.entry: Rice, canned tuna fish, and tea leaves

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ONPCRs: fluorescent oxygen-doped, nitrogen-rich, photoluminescent polymer carbon nanoribbons.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in water and various drinks.

head.ce:title: A sensitive and selective sensing platform based on CdTe QDs in the presence of l-cysteine for detection of silver, mercury and copper ions in water and various drinks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, Institute for Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry
Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, The Second Hospital of Huangshi, Huangshi 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water soluble CdTe quantum dots (QDs) have been prepared simply by one-pot method using potassium tellurite as stable tellurium source and thioglycolic acid (TGA) as stabilizer. The fluorescence of CdTe QDs can be improved 1.3-fold in the presence of l-cysteine (Cys), however, highly efficiently quenched in the presence of silver or mercury or copper ions. A sensitive and selective sensing platform for analysis of silver, mercury and copper ions has been simply established based on CdTe QDs in the presence of l-cysteine. Under the optimum conditions, excellent linear relationships exist between the quenching degree of the sensing platform and the concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ranging from 0.5 to 40ngmL−1. By using masking agents of sodium diethyldithiocarbamate (DDTC) for Ag+ and Cu2+, NH4OH for Ag+ and Hg2+ and 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) for Hg2+ and Cu2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ can be exclusively detected in coexistence with other two ions, and the detection limits (3σ) were 0.65, 0.063 and 0.088ngmL−1 for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. This effective sensing platform has been used to detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ in water and various drinks with satisfactory results.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer
fluorescent oxygen-doped, nitrogen-rich, photoluminescent polymer carbon nanoribbons

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, mercury and silver are widely used in the electrical industry, metallurgy and photographic materials, and unavoidably produce various types of wastes, polluting air, water and soil. Especially, mercury can be translated into greater toxicity of methylmercury by the action of microorganisms, which can damage the central nervous system, and lead to movement disorders, spasm, paralysis, language and hearing disorders and even death (Costas-Mora, Romero, Lavilla, & Bendicho, 2014). Although copper is one of the essential trace elements in the human body, it is hazardous at a higher level and its accumulation can cause heart, skin and pancreas diseases (Liu, Liu, Zang, & Liu, 2015). An excess amount of silver is also toxic to human health, including argyrosis, liver and kidney damage and changes to blood cells (Jiao, Zhang, Liang, Peng, & Lin, 2014). Therefore, development of an efficient and sensitive method for detection of trace Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ in real world samples is particularly important.

Analytical techniques, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (Trindade, Dantas, Lima, Ferreira, & Teixeira, 2015), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) (Losev, Buyko, Trofimchuk, & Zuy, 2015), stripping voltammetry (Tukur, Yusof, & Hajian, 2015), X-ray fluorescence spectrometry (Sitko et al., 2015) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Wang, Wu, Chen, He, & Hu, 2015) are usually used to detect heavy metal ions. However, high cost, complicated sample pretreatment, large sample volumes and time-consuming are often required by most of them. Accordingly, it is a great challenge to develop a new method for detection of silver, mercury and copper ions. Fluorescence assays, hence, have attracted the attentions of analytical scientists owing to the advantages of simple, sensitivity, selectivity and economy (Huang, Niu, Wang, Lv, & Zeng, 2013).

For fluorescence detection methods, selection of an appropriate probe is an important strategy in the elaboration of an analytical procedure, and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have proved to be especially effective as a result of high photoluminescence efficiency, large Stokes shift, broad absorption spectra, narrow emission spectra, resistance against photobleaching, long fluorescence lifetime and adjustable dimensions (Ding, Shen, Sun, Sun, & Liu, 2014). Ding et al. successfully developed a simple and accurate fluorescent method for Cu2+ detection using glutathione functionalized ZnSe QDs. Wang et al. developed a near-infrared-emitting QDs with highly luminescence, good stability and low toxicity for detection of Hg2+ (Wang, Li, Shao, & Han, 2015). A thiolactic acid capped blue-emitting ZnS QDs were applied for efficient detection of silver ions by Mandal et al. (Mandal, Dandapat, & De, 2012). However, reports on a sensing platform based on semiconductor QDs for detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+, respectively, are few.

The aim of this work is to synthesize simply CdTe QDs by one-pot method using potassium tellurite as a stable tellurium source and thioglycolic acid as stabilizer, and to explore the possibility of using it as a sensing platform for determination of Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+ in the presence of l-cysteine (Cys), respectively.

Analytical grade of Cd(CH3COO)2·H2O, NaBH4, NaOH, HgSO4, AgNO3, H3PO4, CH3COOH, H3BO3 and Cu(NO3)2 (Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Company), K2TeO3 (Aladdin), thioglycolic acid (TGA) (Alfa Aesar), Cys (Shanghai Boao Biotechnology Co. Ltd.), 1-(2-Pyridylazo)-2-naphthol (PAN) (Shanghai Shanpu Chemical Co. Ltd.), sodium diethyldithiocarbamate (DDTC) (Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co. Ltd.) was used in this study. For pH adjustment, a series of Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer solutions containing H3PO4, CH3COOH and H3BO3 each with concentrations at 40mmolL−1 were used. Double distilled water (DDW) was used throughout all the experiments.

The pH measurements were performed by a PB-10 exact digital pH meter (Sartorius, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Nicolet 5700 IR spectrometer in the range of 400–4000cm−1 (Nicolet, USA). The fluorescence measurements were recorded on a LS45 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra was conducted on a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of water-soluble CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Monodisperse CdTe QDs were synthesized similar to literature (Wu, Dou, Zhang, & Zhang, 2012). Specifically, 18.0μL TGA was added into 0.2mmol Cd(CH3COO)2·H2O dissolved in 50.0mL DDW under stirring, followed by adjusting the pH to 10.5 by dropwise addition of 1.0molL−1 NaOH solution. Then the resulting solution was placed in a three-necked flask for stirring 5min. After that, 0.04mmol of K2TeO3 dissolved in 50.0mL DDW and 80mg NaBH4 were successively added into the mixture solution. The resulting mixture was proceeded for stirring another 5min, then refluxed at 100°C for 2h under open-air conditions. After mixing with 100mL acetone, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 5min. Finally, QDs were collected by removing the supernatant, dried under vacuum for 24h and then dissolved in DDW at a concentration of 0.5mgmL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, 35μL CdTe QDs, 100μL 100μgmL−1 Cys, 625μL 40mmolL−1 B-R buffer (pH 7.0) were mixed at room temperature for 10min. The mixture were added into different concentrations of Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, respectively, and then diluted to 5.0mL with DDW. After 10min, fluorescence intensity of the resulting solution was recorded following excitation at 458nm.
To evaluate the performance of the sensing platform for detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, the present method was used for analysis of water, tea infusion, soda water, orange juice and red wine.

Water samples (Sanjiao Lake and Qingshan Lake, Hubei Normal University) were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min, filtered through a 0.45μm membrane to remove large particles of impurities prior to analysis.

Tea infusion was generally infused tea leaves three times. Typically, 2.0g three kinds of tea leaves (purchased from local supermarket) were weighed, soaked in 20mL boiling DDW (95°C) for 5min, filtered through a 0.45μm membrane. After collecting infusion, the residues were re-leached two times, following the above procedure. The three infusions were combined for future use.
Soda water (purchased from local supermarket) was put in an ultrasonic bath for 15min to remove air bubbles, filtered through a 0.45μm membrane prior to analysis.
Orange juice and red wine (purchased from local supermarket) was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.45μm membrane prior to analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and properties of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the structural formation of CdTe QDs, FTIR of TGA (a) and TGA-capped CdTe QDs (b) was performed with a Nicolet 5700 FTIR instrument, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 A. As in Fig. 1A-(a), peaks at about 3440cm−1, 1710cm−1 and 2600–2500cm−1 represent the stretching vibration of OH, CO and SH groups, respectively (Bojdys, Müller, Antonietti, & Thomas, 2008). However, in Fig. 1A-(b), the characteristic absorption band of SH at 2600–2500cm−1 disappears and CO at 1700cm−1 moves to short wave number, indicating TGA and CdTe QDs have been successfully linked with sulfur-metal bonds.

Fig. 1B shows a typical fluorescence excitation at 458nm and a strong emission spectra of CdTe QDs at 560nm. The QDs solution is pale yellow under visible light, while emits very intense bright yellow green luminescence under UV light (365nm). The size of CdTe QDs is about 3nm, which is calculated according to an equation reported in literature (Yu, Qu, Guo, & Peng, 2003).

Quantum yield (QY) was calculated according to work (Zhu, Zhao, Chi, & Gao, 2013). Fluorescein dispersed in 0.1molL−1 NaOH (QY 95%) was used as a standard, and the absorbance of CdTe QDs and fluorescein were both kept below 0.05 (Xu et al., 2015). The fluorescence QY for the as-prepared CdTe QDs was 41%.

To confirm the stability of CdTe QDs in the presence of high ionic strength, effect of NaCl ranging from 0 to 50mgmL−1 on the fluorescence intensities was tested in a 40mmolL−1 B-R buffer solution (pH 7.0). There were no obvious changes when NaCl was less than 10mgmL−1, and had little effects when it was over 10mgmL−1. These findings suggest that TGA-capped CdTe QDs have the potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions since actual samples generally do not have such a large concentration of salt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishing and optimization the sensing platform (Cys-CdTe QDs)
Effect of amino acid on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs, Cys, glycine (Gly), l-tyrosine (Tyr) and l-cystine (Cys-Cys) were tested and the results are shown in Supplementary Fig. S1. As seen, Gly, Tyr and Cys-Cys have no obvious effect except for Cys. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs can be improved 1.3-fold in the presence of Cys. This is because only Cys contains a thiol group, which possesses a strong affinity towards Cd2+ on the surface of CdTe QDs (Li, Wang, Ni, & Kokot, 2015). The bonded Cd-S can improve the recombination fluorescence of CdTe QDs by creating more radiative centers at the CdTe/Cd-SR complex and stimulation blocking of nonradiative electron-hole recombination defect sites on the surface of CdTe QDs (Khantaw, Boonmee, Tuntulani, & Ngeontae, 2013).

The concentration of Cys on fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs was tested further and the results are shown in Supplementary Fig. S2. As seen, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs increased rapidly with Cys, and then attained a platform at 2.0μgmL−1. Thus, 2.0μgmL−1 Cys was selected as the optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CdTe QDs volume on the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of CdTe QDs volume (15–45μL) on the fluorescence intensity was studied in presence of 2.0μgmL−1 Cys, and the results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity increased rapidly from 15 to 35μL, and obtained the highest when the volume of CdTe QDs was 35μL. Hence, 35μL CdTe QDs was selected in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and Cys-CdTe QDs was studied. The fluorescence intensity of the both systems was reduced to zero at pH 2.0–4.0, and increased quickly from pH 5.0 to 6.0, then attained the maximum, and finally decreased gradually from pH 8.0 to 12.0, suggesting the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs intensely depends on pH (Supplementary Fig. S3). Highly acidic or alkaline surroundings would negligibly affect the protonation-deprotonation of the Cys-CdTe QDs, leading to the fluorescence intensity declines (Lu et al., 2012). Considering the effect of protonation-deprotonation on Cys-CdTe QDs, pH 7.0 B-R buffer was selected as optimum.

In addition to sensitivity, selectivity is another important parameter for an ideal sensor. Under the optimal conditions, effects of Cr6+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, As5+, Se6+, Se4+, K+, Zn2+, Au3+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and Mn2+ on fluorescence intensity of the sensing platform were researched, respectively, and the results are listed in Fig. 2 (F0 represents fluorescence intensity of the initial sensing platform, and F represents the fluorescence intensity after addition of the quenching component into the initial sensing platform). It is clear that a serious fluorescence quenching was observed for Cys-CdTe QDs system with the addition of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively, indicating the sensing platform shows excellent selectivity toward Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+. Supplementary Fig. S4 shows the fluorescence spectrum of CdTe QDs, Cys-CdTe QDs and Cys-CdTe QDs in the presence of Ag+, Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively. It is shown intuitively the fluorescence effects of Cys-CdTe QDs quenched by Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively.

In order to detect accurately Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively, and eliminate the interference from other two metal ions, chelating agents were tested and the results are shown in Fig. 3 . By using masking agents of DDTC for Ag+ and Cu2+, NH4OH for Ag+ and Hg2+ and PAN for Hg2+ and Cu2+, Hg2+, Cu2+and Ag+ can be exclusively detected in coexistence with other two ions. It demonstrates that Cys-CdTe QDs is an effective fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the sensitivity of the sensing platform, various contents of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions were added to the system for 10min, respectively, and the changes of fluorescence intensity were recorded in Fig. 4 . As shown in Fig. 4, the fluorescence quenching levels of the sensing platform increase with Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ contents and the calibration curves are established by the quenching ratio F0/F versus the concentration of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. Analytical characteristic data of this work are summarized in Supplementary Table S1. The linear ranges of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ are 0.5–40ngmL−1. LODs, calculated as the concentration of analyte that give rise to peak areas with three times of S/N, are 0.65, 0.063 and 0.088ngmL−1 for Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively.

Table 1 compares the analytical characteristics of this work with various methods for detection of Ag+, Hg2+ or Cu2+. As seen, as the same kind of method based on application different fluorescent probes in detection of Ag+, Hg2+ or Cu2+, LODs of this method are lower than those of studies (Cai, Cheng, Wang, & Bao, 2014; Shi et al., 2015; Song et al., 2015; Zhang & Chen, 2014; Zhao et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2014) and close to works (Chen, Zhao, Zhang, & Tao, 2014; Lu et al., 2012). However, as compared with the multiple elements method of ICP-OES, our LODs are still lower than those (Losev et al., 2015; Shoaee, Roshdi, Khanlarzadeh, & Beiraghi, 2012) and close to that (Mashhadizadeh, Amoli-Diva, Shapouri, & Afruzi, 2014). Although, the LODs of this work are inferior to ICP-MS methods (Peng et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015), both of them combined with an extraction procedure. All of these indicate this work is cheaper, simpler, more selective and sensitive. In addition, as the same kind of fluorescence method, the prepared method of CdTe QDs (one-pot) is not only simple, but also three kinds of metal ions (Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+) could be detected by using this sensing platform, while, others usually could determine only one or two kinds of metal ions (Cai et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2014; Ding et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2015; Song et al., 2015; Wang & Ding, 2014; Zhang & Chen, 2014; Zhao et al., 2014; Zong et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions detection by Cys-CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: At present, the following five ways are usually used to explain the mechanisms of fluorescence intensity of QDs quenched by metal ions. They are cation-exchange (Kacmaz et al., 2015), competition of ligands (Wang, Lv, & Hou, 2011), electron transfer (Li et al., 2014), binding with surface ligands and electron transfer (Yu et al., 2015), and inner filter effect (Dong et al., 2012). In our case, the fluorescence intensity of Cys-CdTe QDs not only decreases with the addition of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions, but also the fluorescence wavelength shifts obviously red (Fig. 4). Mechanisms of electron transfer and competition of ligands may explain these phenomena. The red shift of the fluorescence spectrum of Cys-CdTe QDs may illustrate an electron transfer progress occurred between Cys-CdTe QDs and Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions. Competition of ligands may exist among Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ and –S– since the Ksp values of Ag2S (Ksp =2×10−49), HgS (Ksp =4×10−53) and CuS (Ksp =6×10−36) are obviously lower than that of CdS (Ksp =8×10−27), which show Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ have stronger affinities towards –S–.

The practical feasibility of Cys-CdTe QDs as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in water and various drinks was investigated. Table 2 gives the results of standard addition method for analysis of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in water, tea infusion, soda water, orange juice and red wine. The content of copper ions found in the three kinds of tea leaves were 1.38μgg−1, 1.69μgg−1 and 2.68μgg−1, respectively. The data of recovery is between 90.1% and 109%, confirming this sensing platform is potential for detection Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in various matrix.

In this paper, water soluble CdTe QDs with superior dispersibility, strong luminescence and high stability have been prepared simply by one-pot method. The presence of Cys can result in 1.3-fold improvement of fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs. A sensitive and selective Cys-CdTe QDs sensing platform for analysis of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions has been simply established. As compared with some of the references, this sensing platform is cheaper, simpler, more selective and sensitive. In addition, as a kind of fluorescence sensing platform, three kinds of metal ions (Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+) could be detected, respectively, are few. The established method has been successfully used for analysis of Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions in water, tea infusion, soda water, orange juice and red wine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by Education Committee of Hubei Province (D20132501), Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technique (Hubei Normal University) (KL2013M06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2016.06.091.
